does: This paper formalizes the interaction between LLM safety evaluators and defensive trainers as a two-player game, introducing group-theoretic tools to model data augmentation and jailbreak robustness. Using cyclic group transformations on toy domains, the authors analyze how model generalization range determines evaluation outcomes, identifying critical thresholds where evaluators maintain constant miss ratios for linearly many rounds. They complement this theoretical analysis with empirical validation across three model families, examining how locality—whether jailbreak defenses generalize beyond their training distribution—affects robustness to adversarial inputs discovered through iterative auditing.
connects: This work builds on recent concerns about jailbreaking attacks on LLMs and the widespread use of fine-tuning for safety alignment, extending prior research in adversarial robustness and game-theoretic analysis of machine learning systems. It responds to the gap between practical defensive strategies and their theoretical understanding, drawing from classical game theory between attacker and defender as well as mathematical frameworks for analyzing generalization. The use of group actions to formalize data augmentation connects to symmetry-aware learning and invariance-based approaches in representation learning, while the empirical focus on locality aligns with ongoing investigations into distribution shift and generalization boundaries in large language models.
enables: This framework opens pathways for designing provably robust fine-tuning strategies and characterizing the fundamental limits of safety-critical LLM training under adversarial evaluation. It suggests future work on understanding which types of symmetries and augmentations provide meaningful robustness guarantees versus those that create false confidence. The game-theoretic perspective enables analysis of optimal evaluation strategies and adaptive training regimes that account for evaluator behavior. Results on locality dependence could inform architectural choices and training methodologies that broaden generalization of safety properties, potentially moving beyond brittle patch-based defenses toward more principled robustness guarantees.
